serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of stir-fried Green Dragon Vegetable is approximately 150 g, served as a shared side dish alongside rice or other mains.

nutrient_source.title: Chinese Kale (Kai Lan) / Leafy Green Vegetable — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Kale, raw (NDB 11233) and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for leafy greens; adapted for Green Dragon Vegetable (青龙菜) as a closely related Chinese leafy brassica-type green
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Green Dragon Vegetable (青龙菜) is a Chinese leafy green vegetable closely related to Chinese kale (kai lan / gai lan) and other brassica-family leafy greens commonly consumed in Singapore and southern China. No direct entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central under this specific name. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy kale/Chinese kale and FAO/INFOODS SEA leafy green composites. Values represent a stir-fried preparation with minimal oil and seasoning. Energy and macros are consistent with comparable Chinese leafy greens reported in the Singapore HPB nutrient database for kai lan and chye sim.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Non-Starchy Vegetables — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate based on Chinese leafy greens and non-starchy vegetables category
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Green Dragon Vegetable (青龙菜) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 15 based on the established GI range for non-starchy leafy green vegetables (GI 10–20) as documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2008; Foster-Powell K et al., Am J Clin Nutr 2002). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 3.8 × 1.5) / 100 = 0.86, rounded to 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium from stir-frying; high potassium content
hypertension.guidance: Green Dragon Vegetable is low in calories and provides potassium (approximately 296 mg per 100 g), which supports blood pressure regulation; however, restaurant preparations may add sauces or salt that increase sodium content, so patients with hypertension should request reduced-sodium preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~15) and low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of approximately 15 and only 3.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g, Green Dragon Vegetable has a negligible glycemic load and is an excellent choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes seeking to manage postprandial blood glucose levels.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre and antioxidant vitamins
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Green Dragon Vegetable is very low in saturated fat (0.2 g per 100 g), contains no cholesterol, and provides dietary fibre, vitamin C, and vitamin A, all of which support cardiovascular health; it is a heart-healthy vegetable choice when prepared with minimal oil.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Green Dragon Vegetable (青龙菜, Qīng lóng cài) is a Chinese leafy green vegetable popular in Singapore and southern Chinese cuisine. It belongs to the brassica family and is closely related to Chinese kale (kai lan / gai lan) and chye sim. The vegetable features long, slender dark-green stems and tender leaves with a mild, slightly bitter flavour and a crisp-tender texture when cooked. It is most commonly prepared by stir-frying with garlic and a small amount of oil, or blanched and served with oyster sauce. In Singapore, it appears frequently as a side dish in Chinese seafood restaurants and zi char establishments, often ordered alongside rice and protein dishes. Nutritionally, Green Dragon Vegetable is low in calories (approximately 35 kcal per 100 g), low in carbohydrates (3.8 g per 100 g), and provides useful amounts of dietary fibre (2.2 g), vitamin C (41 mg), vitamin A (241 mcg RAE), folate (62 mcg DFE), calcium (72 mg), and iron (1.5 mg). Its very low glycemic index (estimated ~15) makes it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The low saturated fat and zero cholesterol profile support cardiovascular health. Sodium content in restaurant preparations may be elevated due to added sauces; home preparation with minimal seasoning keeps sodium low. The dish is naturally vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, and halal.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Green Dragon Vegetable (青龙菜) was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA under this specific name. Nutrient values are adapted from closely related Chinese leafy brassica greens (kale, kai lan, chye sim) using USDA SR Legacy and FAO/INFOODS SEA data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database non-starchy vegetable category. Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: Green Dragon Vegetable (青龙菜) is a Chinese leafy green vegetable served at The Seafood Market (By House of White Bee Hoon) in Singapore. The field survey listing price of SGD 18.00 reflects a restaurant portion likely served as a shared side dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy kale/Chinese kale (NDB 11233) and FAO/INFOODS SEA leafy green composites as no direct database entry exists for this specific vegetable under this name. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero for a minimally processed plant food with no hydrogenated oils). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero; plant-based food contains no dietary cholesterol). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero; plant-based food with no animal-derived components). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero; leafy green vegetables do not contain meaningful vitamin D). monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy and FAO/INFOODS SEA analogous leafy green data. GI set to 15 as an analogous estimate for non-starchy leafy green vegetables per University of Sydney GI Database category data. Glycemic load per serving = (15 × 3.8 × 1.5) / 100 = 0.86, rounded to 0.9. Sodium of 180 mg per 100 g reflects a typical stir-fried restaurant preparation with light seasoning; home preparation would be lower. Follow-up: if HPB Singapore Foodo adds a specific entry for 青龙菜, this record should be updated to exact match with high confidence.
